2605.00817.title: When LLMs Stop Following Steps: A Diagnostic Study of Procedural Execution in Language Models
2605.00817.paper_summary: Large language models (LLMs) often achieve strong performance on reasoning benchmarks, but final-answer accuracy alone does not show whether they faithfully execute the procedure specified in a prompt. We study this question through a controlled diagnostic benchmark for procedural execution, where models are given a step-wise arithmetic algorithm and two numeric inputs, and must return the final computed value. The benchmark uses simple arithmetic operations but increases complexity through algorithm length and look-back dependencies over intermediate variables. Across 14 models and 55 datasets, average first-answer accuracy drops from 61% on 5-step procedures to 20% on 95-step procedures. Generation-level analysis shows that failures often involve missing answers, premature answers, self-correction after an initial error, under-executed traces, and hallucinated extra steps. These findings suggest that apparent reasoning ability can mask substantial weaknesses in faithful instruction execution.
2605.00817.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型（LLMs）在推理基準上通常表現出色，但僅僅依賴最終答案的準確性並不能顯示它們是否忠實執行了提示中指定的程序。<lb/>我們通過一個受控的診斷基準來研究這個問題，該基準用於程序執行，模型被給予一個逐步的算術算法和兩個數值輸入，並必須返回最終計算值。<lb/>該基準使用簡單的算術運算，但通過算法長度和對中間變量的回顧依賴性來增加複雜性。<lb/>在14個模型和55個數據集上，平均首次答案的準確率從5步程序的61%下降到95步程序的20%。<lb/>生成級別的分析顯示，失敗通常涉及缺失答案、過早答案、在初始錯誤後的自我修正、執行不完全的痕跡和幻覺的額外步驟。<lb/>這些發現表明，表面上的推理能力可能掩蓋了在忠實指令執行方面的重大弱點。
2605.00817.author: Sailesh Panda et.al.
2605.00817.authors: Sailesh Panda, Pritam Kadasi, Abhishek Upperwal, Mayank Singh
